questions.question: Is 'consistency' a single on/off property?
options: Yes — data is either consistent or not
No — it's a spectrum from strong (linearizable) to eventual, with useful models in between
Yes — but only for SQL databases
No — there are exactly two models
questions.explanation: Consistency is a spectrum. At the strong end, the system acts like a single copy everyone sees instantly; at the weak end, replicas converge eventually. Causal consistency, read-your-writes, and monotonic reads sit usefully in between.

questions.question: Why not just use the strongest consistency (linearizability) everywhere?
options: It is illegal in distributed systems
It requires coordination between replicas, adding latency to every operation and reducing availability during partitions
It corrupts data
It only works on a single machine
questions.explanation: Strong consistency needs replicas to coordinate (agree) before answering, which costs latency on every operation and availability under partitions (PACELC). Most data — feeds, counts, presence — doesn't need it, so paying that cost everywhere is wasteful.

questions.question: What does linearizability guarantee?
options: Reads are eventually correct
The system behaves like a single copy: once a write completes, every later read sees that write or a newer one, never an older value
Each client sees its own writes but others may not
Writes are batched for efficiency
questions.explanation: Linearizability makes the distributed system behave as if there's one copy, with each operation taking effect atomically in real-time order. After a write completes, no subsequent read by anyone can return a stale value. It's the strongest and most expensive single-object model.

questions.question: A user refreshes a page and the view count goes from 100 down to 98. Which guarantee would prevent this?
options: Read-your-writes
Monotonic reads — a client's successive reads never return an older value than one already seen
Linearizability is the only fix
Partition tolerance
questions.explanation: Seeing a value go backward (100 then 98) violates monotonic reads, typically caused by hitting a more-lagged replica on the second read. Pinning the client to one replica (or one at least as fresh) provides monotonic reads cheaply, without full strong consistency.

questions.question: What does eventual consistency actually promise?
options: That data is unreliable and often wrong
That if writes stop, all replicas will converge to the same value — though reads may be stale until they do
That every read always sees the latest write
That writes are never lost under any circumstance
questions.explanation: Eventual consistency guarantees convergence: absent new writes, replicas will agree eventually. Reads can return stale data in the meantime, and a conflict-resolution rule (last-write-wins, CRDTs) settles concurrent writes. For tolerant data it's correct, highly available, and fast — not 'unreliable'.
